role_summary: SNCC national office staff (1964-1966), based in Atlanta and organizing Northern student support under Executive Director James Forman.
ai_reading: The embedding places Robinson among a tight cluster of SNCC organizers whose racial composition was unusual for the Southern movement: Timothy Jenkins (entry 120, count 210), Martha Prescod Norman Noonan (entry 83), Maria Varela (entry 81), Dottie Zellner (entry 30), and Phil Hutchings (entry 98). Several of these were white SNCC staff or coalition-bridging figures; the embedding identifies her as part of that subgroup rather than placing her with all SNCC staff equally. On the Individual Conscience vs Collective Discipline axis she lands at -0.44, toward Individual Conscience, an unexpected pull for someone whose institutional role at the Atlanta SNCC office was organization-building (allocations, fundraising, the Friends of SNCC chapter network), all collective-discipline work; the embedding seems to read her testimony as reflective and conscience-narrative rather than as the operational language her job would have produced.
biographical_paragraph: Betty Garman Robinson came to civil rights organizing from the Northern campus left, not from a Southern church basement [src: 2]. As a Skidmore College undergraduate in the late 1950s she ran National Student Association forums pushing back against the era's domestic anti-communist climate, and when the Greensboro sit-ins broke open the spring of 1960 she joined Woolworth's solidarity pickets in February [src: 2]. Graduate school at Berkeley put her inside Students for a Democratic Society [src: 2]. In March 1964 she moved cross-country to Atlanta to take a desk in the SNCC national office, working under James Forman's direction on money, on student outreach across the North, and on stitching together the Friends of SNCC chapter network; Casey Hayden was a colleague [src: 2]. Born January 8, 1939 in Pleasantville, New York, Garman Robinson died October 11, 2020 [src: 2]. Her oral history forms entry 7 of the Civil Rights History Project at the Library of Congress [src: 1].
movement_context: The Friends of SNCC chapter network Garman Robinson coordinated from the Atlanta national office was the principal fundraising and political-support infrastructure that financed SNCC's southern field operations through the 1964 Freedom Summer and the 1965 Selma campaign [src: 3]. James Forman's institutional design (he served as SNCC Executive Director from October 1961 through May 1966) placed the Atlanta office at the center of a small-donor financial-engine model: roughly two hundred Friends of SNCC chapters at the network's mid-1960s peak operated in northern campus communities, Jewish liberal-left congregations, and Quaker peace-movement networks, raising the operating budget that kept the southern field staff on monthly stipends in the ten-to-twenty-dollar range [src: 4]. Garman Robinson's white-SNCC-staff position (her oral history is among the catalog's seven white-SNCC-staff testimonies the AI's-reading observation places her structurally adjacent to) was the racial configuration that the November 1966 Kingston Springs, Tennessee SNCC staff meeting (the meeting at which Stokely Carmichael was elected SNCC chair, the Black Power program was adopted, and the position of white SNCC staff was formally reconsidered) ended; her departure from the SNCC payroll in 1966 followed that institutional reorganization [src: 4].
legacy_and_reception: After her SNCC tenure Garman Robinson moved to Baltimore in 1970 with her husband Reginald Robinson (a SNCC field secretary in Maryland's Eastern Shore campaign), where she organized for half a century in Black-and-white community-organizing coalition work, education-equity advocacy, and local Baltimore housing-justice campaigns [src: 2]. She maintained continuing public correspondence with the SNCC alumni network and was active in the SNCC Legacy Project that produced the SNCC Digital Gateway documentary platform at Duke University; the SNCC Digital Gateway records her as a continuing-organizer voice in the institutional memory work the SNCC Legacy Project anchors [src: 2]. She died October 11, 2020 in Baltimore at eighty-one, three weeks before the November 2020 general election that her continuing organizing work had pointed toward [src: 2].

gallery.caption: James Forman, SNCC Executive Director from October 1961 through May 1966 and Garman Robinson's direct supervisor during her March 1964 to 1966 tenure in the SNCC Atlanta national office. Photographed for the Portland Press Herald, May 9, 1969.

gallery.license: Public domain (U.S. publication 1931-1977 without copyright notice)

gallery.alt: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964
gallery.caption: SNCC Mississippi Freedom Summer Project recruitment broadside, April 1964. Garman Robinson had taken her desk at the SNCC Atlanta national office a month earlier, in March 1964; the Friends of SNCC chapter network she coordinated through the spring of 1964 was the principal Northern-campus channel through which Freedom Summer recruits learned about and applied to the project. Held by the Mississippi Department of Archives and History.
